content: The analysis uses survey-weighted multivariable logistic regression with three pooled NHANES cycles (2007-2012) and 6-year combined weights. Blood VOC concentrations (log-transformed) serve as the exposure variable, with 41 VOCs screened and those with >= 10% detection frequency retained. Airflow obstruction is classified using both pre- and post-bronchodilator spirometry following GOLD criteria: non-reversible obstruction (post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC < 0.70, consistent with COPD) versus reversible obstruction (post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC >= 0.70, consistent with asthma). This dual classification is a methodological innovation — most prior studies used only pre-bronchodilator spirometry. Models adjust for age, poverty income ratio, BMI, pack-years of smoking (log-transformed), sex, race/ethnicity, current/past smoking status, environmental tobacco smoke exposure (serum cotinine), and physical activity. Complex survey design is handled using NHANES sampling weights (divided by 3 for 6-year combination), masked variance PSU (SDMVPSU), and strata (SDMVSTRA).
keywords: Survey-weighted multivariable logistic regression
Three pooled NHANES cycles (2007-2012) with 6-year combined weights
41 blood VOCs screened by headspace SPME-GC-IDMS
Non-reversible vs reversible airflow obstruction classification (post-bronchodilator)
GOLD criteria: pre-BD FEV1/FVC < 0.70 with post-BD confirmation
Log-transformed blood VOC concentrations as continuous exposures

content: Among 5,988 adults, blood xylenes were significantly associated with non-reversible airflow obstruction: m-/p-xylene (OR: 1.18, 95% CI: 1.04-1.33) and o-xylene (OR: 1.19, 95% CI: 1.02-1.39). Trihalomethanes — chlorinated water disinfection byproducts — showed novel associations with non-reversible obstruction: bromodichloromethane (OR: 1.23, 95% CI: 1.04-1.45) and dibromochloromethane (OR: 1.27, 95% CI: 1.06-1.51). Blood styrene was uniquely associated with reversible (asthma-like) airflow obstruction (OR: 1.51, 95% CI: 1.02-2.23) but not with non-reversible obstruction. This differential pattern — xylenes and trihalomethanes with COPD-like obstruction, styrene with asthma-like obstruction — represents the first evidence that different VOCs may preferentially affect distinct respiratory disease phenotypes.
keywords: m-/p-xylene OR: 1.18 (1.04-1.33) for non-reversible obstruction
o-xylene OR: 1.19 (1.02-1.39) for non-reversible obstruction
Bromodichloromethane OR: 1.23 (1.04-1.45) — trihalomethane/chlorinated water byproduct
Dibromochloromethane OR: 1.27 (1.06-1.51) — trihalomethane/chlorinated water byproduct
Styrene OR: 1.51 (1.02-2.23) for reversible obstruction only
Differential VOC-obstruction phenotype pattern (COPD-type vs asthma-type)

content: Multiple strategies address confounding by tobacco smoke — the dominant source of VOC exposure: (1) inclusion of both self-reported smoking status (current/past/never) AND log-transformed pack-years as separate covariates captures both current exposure and cumulative dose; (2) serum cotinine as an objective biomarker of environmental tobacco smoke (ETS) exposure, beyond self-report; (3) formal interaction tests examining whether VOC-obstruction associations differ by smoking status (never vs ever smokers); (4) sensitivity analyses excluding current smokers entirely to isolate non-smoking VOC exposure effects. This multi-layered approach to smoking confounding control is critical because many blood VOCs (especially toluene, benzene, xylenes) have both tobacco and non-tobacco sources. The finding that trihalomethane associations are independent of smoking status is particularly robust, as trihalomethanes derive from chlorinated water rather than combustion sources.
keywords: Dual smoking adjustment: self-reported status + log-transformed pack-years
Objective ETS biomarker (serum cotinine) as additional covariate
Effect modification tests by smoking status (interaction terms)
Sensitivity analysis excluding current smokers
Trihalomethane associations independent of smoking (non-combustion source)
Multi-layered tobacco confounding control strategy

content: The study reveals three clinically significant insights: (1) xylenes — ubiquitous solvents in paints, adhesives, and vehicle emissions — are associated with non-reversible (COPD-like) obstruction at ambient exposure levels, extending occupational health findings to the general population; (2) trihalomethanes formed during water chlorination are a previously unrecognized risk factor for airflow obstruction, with implications for drinking water treatment policy; (3) the differential association of styrene with reversible but not non-reversible obstruction suggests a pro-inflammatory/bronchospastic mechanism (asthma pathway) rather than a fibrotic/emphysematous mechanism (COPD pathway). The authors note that blood VOC levels reflect integrated personal exposure from all sources — indoor air, outdoor air, occupational, water, consumer products — making this a more comprehensive exposure assessment than ambient air monitoring alone. The cross-sectional design limits causal inference, and single blood measurements may not capture chronic exposure patterns.
keywords: Xylenes: ambient-level exposure associated with COPD-like obstruction
Trihalomethanes: novel water chlorination byproduct respiratory risk
Styrene: reversible obstruction suggests bronchospastic (asthma) mechanism
Blood VOCs as integrated personal exposure biomarkers (all sources)
Translation from occupational to general population exposure levels
Cross-sectional design limits causal inference

content: Published in the European Respiratory Journal (IF: 24.3), the manuscript adheres to STROBE guidelines for cross-sectional studies. Participant selection from NHANES 2007-2012 is documented with clear inclusion criteria (adults >= 20 years with valid spirometry and blood VOC measurements, n=5,988). The complex survey design and 6-year combined weighting procedures are specified. All odds ratios are reported with 95% confidence intervals. The distinction between pre- and post-bronchodilator spirometry definitions is clearly explained. Study limitations are acknowledged including cross-sectional design, single blood VOC measurement, and potential for residual confounding despite extensive covariate adjustment. Funded by NIEHS (P30ES006096) and NIDA/NIEHS grants, with no conflicts of interest reported.
keywords: STROBE adherence for cross-sectional study
Published in ERJ (IF: 24.3, top-3 respiratory journal)
Participant flow documented (n=5,988 from 3 NHANES cycles)
All ORs with 95% confidence intervals
Pre- and post-bronchodilator spirometry criteria clearly specified
NIEHS/NIDA funded, no conflicts of interest
